title: The Role of the Consultant and Nurse in Modern Hair Restoration
moduleOverview: Teaching Manual Volume 1, Week 1: the modern hair restoration clinic as a coordinated system; the consultant and nurse as clinical-support professionals who inform, structure, document, and escalate within a doctor-led pathway. Emphasises patient safety, trust, accurate handover, and why excellence is integration—not conversion pressure.
learningOutcomes: Explain how first-contact roles influence safety, trust, and efficiency before the doctor encounter
Map the patient journey and identify failure points where communication or process breaks down
Differentiate support tasks from diagnosis, suitability decisions, and informed consent
Relate role clarity to sustainable reputation and lower medico-legal risk for the organisation

assessmentRules.description: Module completion requires the linked assessment pass.

title: Foundations of Hair and Scalp Science
moduleOverview: Teaching Manual Volume 1, Week 3: hair follicle and cycle foundations, density versus calibre, and pattern vocabulary for plain-language education and neutral documentation. Positions science literacy as supporting trust and handover—not as a substitute for medical interpretation. Ethics and scope themes from Volume 1 Week 2 are taught in the Level 2 Ethics and Scope module.
learningOutcomes: Describe follicle structure and the hair growth cycle in accessible patient language
Explain density and calibre differences and why thinning can coexist with visible shedding
Use educational pattern categories only as handover context, never as independent diagnosis
Produce doctor-ready notes that separate observation from clinical interpretation

title: Common Hair Loss Presentations Seen in Clinic
moduleOverview: Teaching Manual Volume 1, Week 4: common hair loss presentations, red flags, psychosocial impact, and disciplined uncertainty. Trains descriptive pattern literacy and escalation judgment while keeping all clinical conclusions with the doctor. Cross-links to Ethics and Scope (Volume 1 Week 2) for boundary language under pressure.
learningOutcomes: Describe common presentations using neutral, professional language suitable for handover
Identify red-flag contexts that require escalation rather than reassurance
Differentiate routine clinician review from higher-urgency pathways per protocol
Support patients emotionally without offering diagnostic or treatment certainty

assessmentRules.description: Pass the linked image and scenario assessment.

title: Consultation Structure and Clinical Information Gathering
moduleOverview: Teaching Manual Volume 2, Week 5: consultation structure and history taking as applied practice—beginning, middle, and end; professional opening; clinically useful history; listening for goals, fears, and assumptions; and notes that support doctor handover and quality review. Safety and trust improve when structure precedes solution-selling.
learningOutcomes: Run a bounded, repeatable consultation arc that orients the patient and protects role clarity
Capture history that reflects onset, progression, context, and caution points without leading
Record underlying concerns that affect expectations and clinician preparation
Produce factual, handover-ready documentation rather than promotional summaries

assessmentRules.description: Pass the assessment and submit the linked practical task.

title: Candidate Screening and Suitability Support
moduleOverview: Teaching Manual Volume 2, Week 6: screening and suitability support—straightforward versus cautionary versus high-priority flows, documenting caution flags, donor humility, emotional and behavioural signals, and professional deferral. Links directly to patient safety, auditability, and intelligent doctor handover.
learningOutcomes: Classify cases at support level for workflow risk and document rationale without diagnostic claims
Surface suitability concern themes for clinician review, including expectations and emotional drivers
Avoid premature feasibility, graft, or outcome talk before appropriate review
Escalate or defer calmly when commercial tempo conflicts with caution

assessmentRules.description: Pass screening support items with mandatory red-flag performance.

title: Ethics, Consent Support, and Scope of Practice
moduleOverview: Teaching Manual Volume 1, Week 2 (delivered here in Level 2): legal-ethical scope, professional boundaries, autonomy and non-maleficence in daily practice, consent support versus clinical consent, confidentiality, honest marketing, and refusing unsafe instructions. Foundation modules 1–3 already covered Volume 1 Weeks 1, 3, and 4 in LMS order—this module deepens the ethical spine of the consultant/nurse role.
learningOutcomes: State scope limits for non-doctor staff and why they protect patients and clinicians
Apply core ethical principles to language, timing, documentation, and escalation
Separate consent support from doctor-led informed consent for treatment
Identify and escalate conflicts between commercial pressure and safe practice

assessmentRules.description: Mandatory pass required on ethics and scope domains.

title: Communication Excellence and Trust-Based Consultation
moduleOverview: Teaching Manual Volume 2, Week 7: communication mastery and expectation management—language as a safety tool, emotional drivers, realism without negativity, risky phrase replacement, and ethical objection handling. Supports consent quality, trust when limits exist, and consistent documentation of patient understanding gaps.
learningOutcomes: Align tone and content with scope-safe, evidence-honest clinic standards
Name and respond to emotional subtext without making clinical promises
Set expectations using measured wording that avoids absolute guarantees
Handle hesitation and objections without pressure tactics

assessmentRules.description: Communication scenario and practical task are both required.

title: Non-Surgical Treatment Education
moduleOverview: Teaching Manual Volume 2, Week 8 (non-surgical and pathway focus in this module): responsible treatment education—pathway sequencing, non-surgical categories, medication awareness without prescribing, and care framed as collaboration not transaction. Surgical overview content from the same manual week continues in Surgical Pathway Education (module 9).
learningOutcomes: Explain non-surgical options with honest timelines, maintenance, and evidence boundaries
Separate education from prescribing and from doctor-led treatment choice
Present staged pathways (support, review delay, combination) without defaulting to surgery hype
Escalate when questions need prescriber or surgeon authority

title: Surgical Education for Non-Doctors
moduleOverview: Mature bridge after non-surgical education (Volume 2 Week 8) and before documentation and handover (Volume 3 Week 9): you explain the surgical pathway as staged process, logistics, and expectation hygiene—never as independent planning, suitability, graft mathematics, or anaesthetic/medical advice. Patients should reach the doctor with clearer questions, not premature decisions.
learningOutcomes: Describe typical pathway stages (e.g. assessment, planning, procedure day, early recovery themes) using general, patient-friendly language aligned to clinic SOP
Separate process education from clinical authority: defer graft design, technique choice, medical fitness, and consent to the treating team
Recognise escalation triggers when patients request confirmation, comparison shopping framed as clinical verdicts, or consent/booking pressure before doctor review
Prepare neutral documentation hooks so handover and Volume 3 evidence work are not undermined by front-line overreach

title: Clinical Photography, Documentation, and Doctor Handover
moduleOverview: Teaching Manual Volume 3, Week 9: clinical photography, objective documentation, doctor handover, and evidence integrity—turning encounters into repeatable, auditable case files that reduce clinician load and ambiguity.
learningOutcomes: Apply standard photographic discipline and honest scalp visibility for clinical review
Write neutral notes that separate report, observation, goals, and open questions
Produce concise handovers with aligned images and focused doctor questions
Detect and correct common integrity failures (missing angles, vague labels, mismatch, informal chat as record)

assessmentRules.description: Pass documentation assessment and submit a faculty-reviewed handover task.

title: Pre-Operative Coordination and Patient Preparation
moduleOverview: Teaching Manual Volume 3, Week 10: pre-operative coordination—sequenced instructions, checklist discipline, anxiety versus clinical uncertainty, proactive confirmation, and documentation of late concerns so treatment teams are not surprised.
learningOutcomes: Run readiness workflows that reduce cancellations and day-of friction
Escalate gaps, disclosures, or misunderstandings before treatment day
Communicate preparation calmly without implying medical clearance
Align with clinic reminder cadence and escalation prompts (SOP-dependent)

title: Post-Operative Support, Follow-Up, and Escalation
moduleOverview: Teaching Manual Volume 3, Week 11 (follow-up strand): routine recovery reassurance, continuity, documented interactions, and early escalation when recovery may diverge—separating emotional distress from clinical urgency where training demands.
learningOutcomes: Support follow-up with structured contact expectations and thorough documentation
Escalate when symptoms, tempo, or uncertainty exceed routine support boundaries
Avoid diagnosing or closing clinical concerns from the front line
Prepare for difficult and dissatisfaction conversations in the next module

title: Difficult Consultations and High-Risk Scenarios
moduleOverview: Teaching Manual Volume 3, Week 11 (difficult cases strand) plus high-risk recovery judgement: dissatisfaction, distress, complaint risk, and ethical pressure—handled without minimisation, argument, or outcome promises; escalation as professionalism.
learningOutcomes: De-escalate ethically while routing clinical and governance review
Document difficult interactions with factual precision
Identify vulnerability, coercion, and complication concern patterns
Protect trust and safety under commercial or interpersonal pressure

assessmentRules.description: Communication and ethics performance must both pass.

title: Quality Assurance, Audit, and Clinic Standards
moduleOverview: Teaching Manual Volume 3, Week 12 (systems and QA): consultation systems, audit loops, continuous improvement, and leadership behaviours that raise standards without formal title—linking records, templates, and communication to governance.
learningOutcomes: Describe system features that reduce harmful variation
Apply audit thinking to notes, photos, language, and escalation patterns
Champion standard tools and ethical escalation culture
Route systemic issues through proper quality improvement channels

title: Final Competency Assessment
moduleOverview: Teaching Manual Volume 3, Week 12 (final integration): capstone simulating real clinic flow—intake, concerns, communication, handover, escalation judgement—with faculty review against mandatory safety domains and professional coherence across the certificate.
learningOutcomes: Demonstrate integrated applied judgement across the full support pathway
Justify documentation and escalation choices with scope-safe reasoning
Meet programme pass and mandatory domain requirements
Show mature communication that is usable in practice, not memorised filler

assessmentRules.description: Capstone pass, faculty review, and competency confirmation are all required.
